stories.title: Flipping the Script on “Safe” Haven Bonds
stories.excerpt: Executive Summary The war in Iran has scrambled traditional views of safety in fixed income, pushing investors out of long‑duration Treasuries and broad investment‑grade benchmarks and into floating‑rate, senior‑secured credit. From February 28 through early July, the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN) returned just over 3% on a total‑return basis, compared with losses of about 5% to 6% for long‑Treasury proxies such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) and mildly negative or flat performance for core bond indexes. War Reshapes “Safe” Bonds A sector‑level review using liquid ETFs shows the divergence clearly. Since the attacks began on February 28, BKLN’s price and income have produced a total return slightly above 3%, more than double the roughly 1.5% gains in dedicated floating‑rate note strategies such as FLRN and short‑maturity high‑yield funds like SJNK over the same window. Long‑duration Treasuries, by contrast, have fallen around 5% in price terms, as represented by TLT’s performance. Core, investment‑grade exposure, proxied by Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND), has also been modestly negative as rising yields offset coupon income. These moves map closely to changing macro expectations. Market‑implied inflation breakevens and policy‑rate futures have shifted toward a higher‑for‑longer profile since late February, while concerns about rising U.S. federal debt levels have intensified, collectively undermining the traditional safe‑haven role of long Treasuries and boosting demand for floating‑rate credit. Why Floating-Rate Credit Is Winning BKLN holds floating‑rate, senior‑secured loans rated below investment grade and currently offers a trailing 12‑month distribution yield in the mid‑single digits, with a portfolio duration close to zero because coupons reset with short‑term benchmarks. In the current environment, investors appear willing to accept additional credit risk in exchange for coupons that adjust upward if the Fed hikes rates, preserving rea
bullets: Floating-rate senior loans (BKLN) outperformed long Treasuries by ~8.5% since Feb 28 geopolitical shock.
Market shifted to higher-for-longer rates, inflation expectations, and federal debt concerns.
Institutional capital fleeing safe-haven Treasuries into floating-rate credit misprices risk for development finance.
stories.summary: Since late February geopolitical tensions, investors abandoned long-duration Treasuries (down ~5–6%) in favor of floating-rate, senior-secured credit (up ~3%), driven by expectations of persistently higher rates and Fed policy uncertainty. Core bond indexes also underperformed. This reflects a structural repricing of what investors view as 'safe,' away from duration and toward floating-rate instruments tied to SOFR.
stories.impact: Rising rate expectations and investor flight from long Treasuries tighten fixed-rate financing availability for multifamily development. Developers should expect refinance and acquisition financing to migrate toward floating-rate structures or hybrid products; construction-period and forward-commitment interest-rate locks become more expensive and harder to obtain. Expect cap rates to reprice upward in all markets as institutional buyers demand higher spreads over SOFR.

stories.title: CPC Mortgage Company Secures $42M to Rehabilitate Michigan Housing Property
stories.excerpt: CPC Mortgage Company, a subsidiary of The Community Preservation Corporation, has structured a $42.1 million loan for the preservation and rehabilitation of Parkside Estates and Demby Terraces, a 315-unit affordable housing community in Inkster, Michigan. Developed by Good Housing Partnership in partnership with the Inkster Housing Commission, the properties will undergo a full rehabilitation and conversion under the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development’s Rental Assistance Demonstration program, transitioning from public housing to Section 8. CPC Mortgage Company provided the permanent financing through a Freddie Mac Targeted Affordable Housing forward commitment structured as a tax-exempt loan. The financing structure includes Huntington Bank, which served as the lead arranger and agent of the construction loan, with Deutsche Bank and Associated Bank as co-lenders. Cinnaire acted as the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit syndicator. The rehabilitation will include extensive upgrades to both the site and residential units. Join the industry’s top multifamily decision-makers at Connect Apartments on September 22 in L.A. Hear from the investors, developers, lenders, owners, and advisors shaping today’s apartment market through candid conversations, networking, and market insights. Register today at www.connectapts2026.com. The post CPC Mortgage Company Secures $42M to Rehabilitate Michigan Housing Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CPC Mortgage secured $42.1M for 315-unit Inkster, Michigan affordable-housing rehab via Freddie Mac.
Public housing-to-Section 8 conversion under HUD's RAD program; Huntington Bank structured construction.
Tax-exempt, permanent financing unlocks affordable product; LIHTC syndication via Cinnaire.
stories.summary: Good Housing Partnership and Inkster Housing Commission secured $42.1 million from CPC Mortgage Company (a Freddie Mac lender) to rehabilitate Parkside Estates and Demby Terraces, a 315-unit public-housing property converting to Section 8. The deal used a tax-exempt structure and included LIHTC syndication, with Huntington Bank and co-lenders funding construction. Full site and unit upgrades are planned.
stories.impact: Demonstrates sustained government appetite for affordable-housing rehab in secondary Midwest markets (Inkster). Freddie Mac forward commitments and RAD conversions remain viable pathways for long-term hold acquisition; Midwest secondary-market affordability play signals investor confidence in demographic stability and stable subsidized rent. Little relevance to for-profit market-rate development.

stories.title: Ice Cream Franchise Coming to Bleecker Street with First NYC Location
stories.excerpt: Scoop N Scootery, a rapidly expanding ice cream franchise with 10 locations across seven states, has selected Bleecker Street for its first New York City location. Lee & Associates NYC arranged the 1,325-square-foot retail lease at 162 Bleecker St., where the company signed a 15-year lease for the ground-floor storefront and plans to open in September 2026. Mark Kapnick, principal at Lee & Associates NYC along with associate director Jordan Cohen, represented landlord Acram Group. Lucas Kooyman of AMPS represented Scoop N Scootery. “New York City is a milestone market for any growing retail brand, and there’s no better place to make that debut than Bleecker Street,” said Kapnick. “Scoop N Scootery wanted a location that immediately establishes its presence in one of the country’s most recognized retail corridors. With exceptional foot traffic, an outstanding mix of food-and-beverage operators and very little available space, Bleecker Street checked every box.” The post Ice Cream Franchise Coming to Bleecker Street with First NYC Location appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Scoop N Scootery opened first NYC location on Bleecker Street; 1,325 SF, 15-year lease.
Franchise expansion signals pedestrian retail confidence in Greenwich Village submarket.
No direct multifamily signal; pure retail tenant story.
stories.summary: Scoop N Scootery, a 10-location ice cream franchise, leased 1,325 SF on Bleecker Street (162 Bleecker St.) for 15 years at an undisclosed rate, opening September 2026. The brand selected the high-foot-traffic Greenwich Village corridor for its NYC flagship, citing density and limited retail availability.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Signals continued retail demand in dense, transit-rich NYC neighborhoods, which indirectly supports renter demand in proximate residential. No supply, capital, cost, or demand implications for apartments.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco (Dallas) entrepreneurs Krishnan and Gounder ordered to pay $400M fraud judgment to Georgetown business.
Scheme involved fake 400-acre luxury waterfront 'District' development; $21M paid to contractor, never built.
Developer fraud; no asset or capital availability impact on market.
stories.summary: A Williamson County jury found Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for defrauding Georgetown-based Energy Commissioning Inc., awarding $400 million in damages. The pair marketed a fake 400-acre 'luxury waterfront District' around San Gabriel River, contracted $21M in build work that never materialized, and engaged in parallel fraud schemes. The defendants were previously subject to SEC restraining orders in 2023.
stories.impact: No direct market impact. Illustrates execution risk and developer reputation vulnerability in Texas land-development space; likely to increase lender and investor due-diligence scrutiny on speculative luxury projects in Hill Country/DFW fringe markets, but no systemic supply or capital effect.

stories.title: Bascom Acquires Buena Park Apartments as Value-Add Deal
stories.excerpt: The Bascom Group acquired Castlewood Park Apartments, a low-density, 183-unit value-add apartment community in Buena Park. The purchase price was $53,125,000, or $290,301 per unit. Brian Eisendrath, Cameron Chalfant, Jesse Zarouk, and Jake Vitta of IPA Capital Markets arranged the acquisition financing, with Brightspire Capital, Inc. providing the acquisition loan. The IPA Investment Sales team led by Kevin Green and Joe Grabiec represented the undisclosed seller. AMC will provide property management services for the community, while SD-Cap will oversee the planned property renovations of the 1963-vintage community. Lee Nguyen, SVP of operations for Bascom, said, “Castlewood already offers many of the features today’s renter values most, like large floorplans, garage parking, abundant green space, and private yards. By thoughtfully modernizing the community while preserving these unique characteristics, we believe Castlewood will continue to be a highly desirable housing option for North Orange County residents.” The post Bascom Acquires Buena Park Apartments as Value-Add Deal appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Bascom Group acquired 183-unit Castlewood Park in Buena Park, CA for $53.1M ($290K/unit).
1963 vintage low-density asset; value-add renovation via SD-Cap; Brightspire provided acquisition financing.
North Orange County market shows appetite for older, suburban garden apartments with large units.
stories.summary: Bascom Group purchased Castlewood Park Apartments (183 units, built 1963) in Buena Park, Orange County for $53.1 million (~$290K/unit). The deal was a value-add acquisition financed by Brightspire Capital; IPA Capital Markets arranged the transaction. The property offers large floorplans, garage parking, and green space; planned renovations will modernize while preserving these characteristics.
stories.impact: Signals continued capital availability for suburban Orange County value-add plays and confidence in large-unit, parking-inclusive Garden product persistence in Southland. $290K/unit valuation reflects modest underwriting in secondary infill market; development risk remains in construction cost inflation and absorption timeline for renovated older stock vs. new supply.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Wildhaven luxury resort/residential development planned on 740 acres in north Denton; $350–450M total value.
152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), 100-key hotel, equestrian, spa, bass fishing pond; 400 acres developed, 300+ preserved.
Nature-centric positioning in high-growth DFW fringe; An Ki Land Development (Sarasota) leading, Noble Homes building first 20 lots.
stories.summary: An Ki Land Development (Sarasota, FL) is developing Wildhaven, a 740-acre master-planned luxury resort community in north Denton, Texas, with total projected value of $350–450 million. The project includes 152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), a 100-key hotel, equestrian facilities, spa, restaurants, and a bass-fishing pond; 400 acres will be developed with 300+ preserved for natural feel. Noble Classic Homes will build the first 20 homes, with additional builders to follow.
stories.impact: Reflects strong demand in DFW luxury for nature-anchored, low-density resort experiences; signals that high-end residential in fast-growing fringe submarkets (Denton has surged in population) can command premium pricing and capital. Minimal direct multifamily impact, but demonstrates capital appetite in North Texas and validates Denton population growth narrative. Large master-planned communities can absorb land that might otherwise support infill multifamily; competes for DFW institutional capital.

stories.title: Walker & Dunlop Arranges Refi for BellWorks Fort Monmouth Campus
stories.excerpt: Walker & Dunlop arranged a $60-million bridge loan to refinance Bell Works Fort Monmouth,a mixed-use campus in Tinton Falls, NJ. The Walker & Dunlop Capital Markets Institutional Advisory team led by Dustin Stolly, Aaron Appel, Sean Reimer, Keith Kurland, Jonathan Schwartz, Adam Schwartz, Ari Hirt and Stanley Cayre arranged the bridge loan provided by Hudson Bay Capital on behalf of a joint venture between Inspired by Somerset and H.I.G. Realty Partners, the real estate platform of H.I.G. Capital. Previously home to the Commvault headquarters, the campus has been redeveloped into a mixed-use destination featuring flexible office spaces, retail, dining, coworking spaces and community-focused amenities. It was developed using Inspired by Somerset Development’s “Metroburb” model for creating a self-contained metropolis in suburbia. The original BellWorks development is in nearby Holmdel, NJ. “Bell Works Fort Monmouth is a highly differentiated mixed-use campus that continues to demonstrate strong leasing momentum and long-term value creation,” said Stolly. “The financing recognizes the strength of the asset’s cash flow today while providing flexibility to support its next phase of growth. We’re grateful to Inspired by Somerset, HIG Capital, and Hudson Bay Capital for their partnership on this transaction.” The post Walker & Dunlop Arranges Refi for BellWorks Fort Monmouth Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Walker & Dunlop arranged $60M bridge loan for Bell Works Fort Monmouth mixed-use campus (Tinton Falls, NJ).
Hudson Bay Capital lender; JV of Inspired by Somerset and H.I.G. Realty; strong leasing momentum cited.
Metroburb suburban mixed-use model funded; office/retail/coworking alternative to traditional CBD.
stories.summary: Walker & Dunlop secured a $60 million bridge loan from Hudson Bay Capital to refinance Bell Works Fort Monmouth, a mixed-use campus in Tinton Falls, NJ, developed by Inspired by Somerset and H.I.G. Capital. The property features flexible office, retail, dining, coworking, and amenities using Inspired by Somerset's 'Metroburb' model. Strong leasing momentum supports the refinance.
stories.impact: Demonstrates institutional capital availability for mixed-use suburban campuses in Northeast; validates suburban flight-to-quality and 'office conversion to living' trend. Not a multifamily development story per se (asset is mixed-use office-centric), but signals investor confidence in suburban 15-minute cities and live-work-play density. May indicate broader reallocation of office capital into experiential mixed-use, constraining pure-play apartment development financing in secondary suburban markets.

stories.title: Shops at Sunset Place owner seeks taxing district to raise $149M
stories.excerpt: The long-awaited redevelopment of the Shops at Sunset Place in South Miami is seeking a nine-figure boost for the mixed-use project’s infrastructure.  Midtown Development, led by founder Alex Vadia, is requesting Miami-Dade County’s approval to establish a special taxing district that would finance $149 million in infrastructure for its planned 10.2-acre Downtown SoMi mixed-use redevelopment at 5701–5795 Southwest 72nd Street.  The developer proposed creating the Downtown SoMi Community Development District, a special-purpose governmental entity that can finance infrastructure and oversee its maintenance within the development. County commissioners are scheduled to consider the proposal during their July 21 meeting.  The district […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Shops at Sunset Place (South Miami) seeking $149M Community Development District for Downtown SoMi mixed-use.
1,513 apartments, 200K+ SF retail, 287 hotel rooms, 65.9K SF office; infrastructure bonds via tax-exempt district.
Infrastructure financing via CDD; construction start 2027, completion Q1 2029; Midtown Development led by Alex Vadia.
stories.summary: Midtown Development (Alex Vadia) is seeking Miami-Dade County approval to establish a Community Development District to issue $149 million in tax-exempt bonds for infrastructure financing of its Downtown SoMi redevelopment on 10.2 acres at the Shops at Sunset Place. The mixed-use project includes 1,513 residential units, 287 hotel rooms, 200K+ SF of retail, 65.9K SF of office, and a 1,300-seat cinema. Infrastructure work slated for 2027–Q1 2029.
stories.impact: CDD structure allows developer to finance horizontal infrastructure without county tax dollars, freeing municipal capital and enabling aggressive development schedule in South Miami. Signal of strong institutional appetite for Miami infill mixed-use; 1,513 units is material supply in SoMi submarket. Watch for competitive pressure on rents and absorption if multiple large projects deliver simultaneously. CDD model increasingly common in Florida growth markets; limits municipal housing-support options for affordable components.

stories.title: Pure Paws Veterinary Care to Relocate to 5K SF at Brooklyn’s 1 Putnam Avenue
stories.excerpt: A pack of Brooklyn veterinarians are taking themselves for a short walk. Pure Paws Veterinary Care is relocating its Brooklyn practice to a larger, nearby location in the Clinton Hill neighborhood. The veterinary office signed a 5,000-square-foot lease at 1 Putnam Avenue, near the intersection of Cambridge Place and Fulton Street. The asking rent for the 10-year lease, signed July 1, was $75 per square foot. Traded first reported the deal. The mixed-use building’s landlord is Abe Betesh. Gabriel and Solomon Betesh of commercial brokerage Momentum Realty Group negotiated the deal on the landlord’s behalf. “We are excited to welcome Pure Paws Veterinary to a new and expanded site to service Clinton Hill,” the brokers said in a joint statement. “We believe in the continued growth of the neighborhood and are proud to play a role in strengthening and enhancing its retail landscape by bringing high-quality businesses to the community.” Pure Paws Veterinary Care currently operates three New York City locations, at 506 West 42nd Street in Manhattan’s Hell’s Kitchen, at 350 Hudson Street in Hudson Square and at 944 Fulton Street in Brooklyn’s Clinton Hill, according to its website. It is unclear when the practice will relocate from its current Clinton Hill address, which is less than a block from its future location.. Babson Real Estate Advisors‘ Richard Bradbury represented the practice in the lease negotiations. Bradbury told Commercial Observer in a statement that Babson was pleased to represent the Pure Paws. “It will give them the opportunity to better serve their loyal and growing customer base for years to come,” Babson said. A representative for Pure Paws did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Pure Paws Veterinary Care leased 5,000 SF at 1 Putnam Avenue, Brooklyn (Clinton Hill) at $75/SF/yr.
10-year lease; Momentum Realty Group negotiated for landlord Abe Betesh; signed July 1.
Indicates stable neighborhood-serving retail demand in Clinton Hill; no multifamily signal.
stories.summary: Pure Paws Veterinary Care signed a 10-year, 5,000-SF lease at 1 Putnam Avenue in Clinton Hill, Brooklyn at $75/SF/year (signed July 1). Momentum Realty Group represented the landlord, Abe Betesh, in the transaction. The practice is relocating from a smaller nearby location to support its multi-location Brooklyn footprint.
stories.impact: Negligible relevance to multifamily. Signals stable neighborhood-retail demand and Clinton Hill gentrification/stabilization; indirectly supports renter viability in the area but offers no supply, capital, or cost implications for apartment development.

stories.title: Bridges Development Group Buys Kingswood Center in Brooklyn for $31M
stories.excerpt: Kingswood Center in Brooklyn’s Midwood neighborhood has a new owner, Commercial Observer has learned. The 229,926-square-foot shopping center at 1630 East 15th Street sold for $31 million to Michael Berfield’s Bridges Development Group, according to broker JLL. The sale marks a steep discount from the $89 million that the shopping center fetched in early 2020, the last time it traded. The three-story, Class B property is currently anchored by T.J. Maxx. A LoopNet listing advertises nearly 100,000 square feet of retail and commercial space available for lease. Kingswood Center was constructed in 2007 and renovated in 2019, according to the listing, and features an on-site parking garage. JLL’s Ethan Stanton, Michael Mazzara and Brendan Maddigan represented the seller, PNC’s Midland Loan Services, in direct negotiations with the buyer. A JLL debt team of Scott Aiese and Alex Staikos secured $34.65 million in acquisition financing for the buyer. The firm declined to disclose the lender. The financing will allow Bridges Development Group to execute a business plan of investing in the property and leasing its vacant space, according to a JLL spokesperson. Midland took possession of the property from its former owner, retail shopping REIT Urban Edge Properties, in 2023. The REIT fell behind on a $66 million commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) loan used to acquire the center in February 2020. Following the ill-timed purchase, the shopping center’s annual net operating income fell to $1.9 million and occupancy declined to 35 percent, The Real Deal reported at the time. Urban Edge Properties still owns the nearby Kingswood Crossing at 1715 East 13th Street, according to its website. Spokespeople for Bridges Development Group and PNC did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bridges Development Group acquired Kingswood Center (Midwood, Brooklyn) for $31M; down 65% from $89M in 2020.
229,926 SF Class B shopping center; T.J. Maxx anchor; ~100K SF retail space available.
Steep price decline signals retail distress; acquisition financing $34.65M secured via JLL.
stories.summary: Bridges Development Group purchased Kingswood Center, a 229,926-SF shopping center in Brooklyn's Midwood neighborhood, for $31 million—a 65% decline from its $89 million sale in early 2020. The Class B property is anchored by T.J. Maxx and features ~100K SF of available retail space. JLL secured $34.65M in acquisition financing for the buyer; the seller was PNC's Midland Loan Services.
stories.impact: Illustrates structural retail-asset weakness and cap-rate expansion in Brooklyn; opportunity for conversion or mixed-use redevelopment on distressed retail footprints. If Bridges pivots toward residential conversion or mixed-use, signals emerging development pipeline for adaptive-reuse apartments in Brooklyn secondary submarkets. Monitor for potential value-add play converting ground-floor retail to residential; however, at current pricing and financing context, conversion underwriting likely marginal.

stories.title: Data Intelligence Firm Curinos Takes 14K SF at Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central
stories.excerpt: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm serving the financial industry, is relocating to Marx Realty’s 10 Grand Central after signing a 14,000-square-foot lease, Commercial Observer has learned. Curinos will move from its current office about two blocks north at 485 Lexington Avenue, though it is unclear when the move will happen. The length of the lease was not disclosed. Asking rents at 10 Grand Central — which is now 98 percent leased, according to the landlord — ranges from $85 to $135 per square foot. Adam Ardise and Rachel Rosenfeld from Cushman & Wakefield represented Curinos in the lease, while JLL’s Mitchell Konsker, Thomas Swartz, Carlee Palmer and Margaux Kelleher represented Marx Realty. Spokespeople for C&W and JLL did not immediately respond to CO’s requests for comment. “The robust leasing activity at 10 Grand Central demonstrates the ongoing appeal of thoughtfully designed workplaces and white glove service that combine to give companies an elevated office experience,” Craig Deitelzweig, CEO of Marx Realty, said in a statement. “Curinos’ decision to relocate within the Grand Central market speaks to the differentiated hospitality-infused experience we’ve created at 10 Grand Central and further strengthens the building’s exceptional tenant roster,” Deitelzweig added. 10 Grand Central is a hospitality-infused office building that offers tenants a variety of amenities, including a reservable house car for transportation, a lounge and cafe on the seventh floor, a 1930s French garden-style outdoor terrace, and a newly opened speakeasy-style restaurant called Highball Ltd. Other tenants at 10 Grand Central include data center developer Edged, bank holding company Merchants Bancorp and research firm Green Street Advisors. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Curinos (financial data firm) leased 14,000 SF at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central (Midtown Manhattan).
Relocating from 485 Lexington Ave (~2 blocks north); rents $85–135/SF; building 98% leased.
No multifamily impact; office leasing momentum in premier Manhattan tower.
stories.summary: Curinos, a global data intelligence firm, leased 14,000 SF at Marx Realty's 10 Grand Central in Midtown Manhattan, relocating from nearby 485 Lexington Avenue. Asking rents at 10 Grand Central range from $85–135/SF; the building is 98% leased. Cushman & Wakefield represented Curinos; JLL represented Marx Realty.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily. Signals ongoing demand for premium Midtown office space among tech/financial tenants; indirectly supports renter demand in Midtown residential but offers no supply, capital, or cost implications for apartments.

stories.title: Iris Escarrá of Greenberg Traurig: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: As co-chair of the land use practice at law firm Greenberg Traurig, Iris Escarrá has worked on a number of high-profile projects. In one recent example, Escarrá was part of the team that won approvals for Miami Freedom Park, the mixed-use development near Miami International Airport that includes Nu Stadium, the 26,700-seat home of Major League Soccer’s Inter Miami CF. Before the stadium opened in April, she led the team that guided the development team through environmental remediation, code compliance and traffic and aviation issues. The following conversation has been edited for length and clarity.   Commercial Observer: Now that Nu Stadium is open and Inter Miami is playing matches there, what lessons did you learn from the project? Iris Escarrá: There’s so many. Some of them I have to cover over drinks. Some of them I can cover here. The stadium is actually one component. It’s 12 acres of a 73-acre complex that’s mixed-use. So while the stadium is the biggest to come in, we have retail coming in, hotel, office, and so forth that we’re also working on. It’s very exciting because that’s what’s actually going to create the whole district. Inter Miami has 17 home games, and they’ll have other events there, concerts, soccer games for high schools, but it’s never going to be a 365-day-a-year building. So it’s really the other uses around it that are going to create the district. And those uses around it are actually what helps with some of the traffic items because the restaurants and the retail are open a few hours before the match, so if you want to go and have lunch before the game, you could do that. If you want to go after the game, go hang out. So what happens is that the mixed-use district around the stadiums is what’s really creating the whole district. When are those other uses going to be completed? We have a few buildings that are in for permitting now, some food and beverage, some entertainment. T
bullets: Iris Escarrá (Greenberg Traurig co-chair) led approvals for Miami Freedom Park (73 acres, mixed-use).
Project includes Nu Stadium (Inter Miami CF, 26.7K seats, opened April 2024) plus retail, hotel, office.
High-profile zoning/entitlement counsel; successful execution of complex mixed-use in South Florida.
stories.summary: Iris Escarrá, co-chair of the land use practice at Greenberg Traurig, led the legal and entitlement team for Miami Freedom Park, a 73-acre mixed-use development near Miami International Airport. The centerpiece Nu Stadium (26,700 seats, home of MLS's Inter Miami CF) opened in April 2024; the broader complex includes retail, hotel, and office components. The project navigated environmental remediation, code compliance, traffic, and aviation issues.
stories.impact: Demonstrates South Florida's capacity to execute large, complex mixed-use projects with sports/entertainment anchors; signals investor/municipal appetite for destination development near MIA. No direct multifamily signal, but validates Miami as growth market willing to rezone/entitle for major projects. Implies zoning flexibility and political will for mixed-use development in South Florida, which can accelerate multifamily approvals in similar contexts.

stories.title: Hochul taps developers to build $1B Hell’s Kitchen towers
stories.excerpt: The state has tapped three New York developers to build two towers with more than 1,100 homes on a publicly owned parking lot in Hell’s Kitchen across from the USS Intrepid museum. Gov. Kathy Hochul selected The Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers and Mural Real Estate to construct the highrises with 1,127 condos and apartments at 621 West 45th Street, Crain’s New York reported.  Plans for the $1 billion project, dubbed Hudson Landing, include two connected towers containing 1.3 million square feet on a 1.2-acre lot across from Pier 86 on the Hudson River. Of the 1,127 units, 108 would be […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gov. Hochul selected Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, Mural Real Estate to build Hudson Landing: 1,127 units.
Hell's Kitchen waterfront (621 W 45th St); $1B project; 108 for-sale condos (28 affordable), 338+ rental units affordable.
State-owned site; zoning override under Hochul's RUSH policy; pedestrian bridge to Intrepid Museum.
stories.summary: New York Gov. Kathy Hochul selected The Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers, and Mural Real Estate to develop Hudson Landing, a $1 billion two-tower project with 1,127 homes on 621 West 45th Street in Hell's Kitchen. The project includes 1.3 million SF across two connected towers on a 1.2-acre state-owned parcel; 108 for-sale condos (28 affordable) and ~338+ rental apartments for households earning 40–130% AMI. The state is overriding local zoning via its Redeveloping Underused Sites as Housing (RUSH) policy to enable residential on a former gas-plant site zoned for light manufacturing.
stories.impact: Hochul's RUSH policy and state zoning override unlock substantial West Side waterfront density; signals political will to override local opposition and unlock prime Manhattan sites for mixed-income housing. Positive supply signal for NYC market (1,127 units) but competitive pressure on Midtown West rents and condo values. Demonstrates state-level intervention to bypass local NIMBYism; watch for replication in other NYC waterfront sites and strong signal that Manhattan infill will remain politically prioritized, supporting long-term rent stability in prime submarkets.

stories.title: Hochul Selects Developers to Build 1,127 Housing Units in Hell’s Kitchen
stories.excerpt: A parking lot near the Intrepid Museum along the Hell’s Kitchen waterfront is about to become housing. Developers Gotham Organization, Fisher Brothers and Mural Real Estate Group have been chosen by Gov. Kathy Hochul to transform the 50,584-square-foot parking lot at 621 West 45th Street into two residential towers containing about 1,127 new homes, the governor’s office announced Wednesday. Crain’s New York Business first reported the news. Hochul announced in February of last year that she wanted to turn the state-owned parking lot along the West Side Highway — which is currently being used by the Intrepid Museum — into a residential development to help dent the city’s housing shortage. “The West Side of Manhattan has a storied history as a vibrant, inclusive community, and I’m committed to strengthening that legacy by building for a more affordable future,” Hochul said in the 2025 announcement. “By transforming this underutilized state-owned property into a dynamic mixed-use development with a significant affordable housing component, we’re taking aim at low housing supply in a high-demand area and building a stronger community for residents in Hell’s Kitchen and beyond.” Gotham, Fisher and Mural will redevelop the lot into two connected residential towers that will feature 1,127 units, with some 338 being set aside as affordable housing for those earning between 40 percent and 130 percent of the area median income, Crain’s reported. The total will include 108 for-sale condo units, with some 28 set aside as affordable housing. “Gotham has spent decades partnering with government, community organizations, and mission-driven institutions on the West Side of Manhattan to deliver housing that serves New Yorkers across the income spectrum, and this project builds on that legacy,” David Picket, CEO of Gotham, said in a statement to Commercial Observer. “This transformative development will deliver hundreds of permanently affordable homes, including homes for
bullets: Hochul/Gotham/Fisher Brothers/Mural selected for Hell's Kitchen 621 W 45th housing: 1,127 units, $1B project.
Parking lot converted to two towers (1.3M SF); 108 condos + 338+ affordable rentals; state zoning override.
Infrastructure: pedestrian bridge to Intrepid, retail, restaurants, ~10K SF park.
stories.summary: Gov. Hochul announced developer selection for Hudson Landing at 621 West 45th Street, a 1,127-unit, $1 billion mixed-use project on a state-owned 50,584-SF parking lot in Hell's Kitchen. Two connected towers will house 1.3 million SF; 108 for-sale condos and rental apartments for households at 40–130% AMI. The state-owned site is being converted from a former gas plant zoned for light manufacturing via Hochul's 2024 Redeveloping Underused Sites as Housing initiative.
stories.impact: Duplicate of article 13 with minor detail variations. Confirms state commitment to unlock underutilized sites for housing; 1,127 units on Manhattan waterfront is significant supply. RUSH policy removes zoning friction and accelerates timelines; competitive pressure on neighboring residential and condo markets. Strong signal of continued institutional support for NYC housing development.

stories.title: PTM Partners, Peacock launch subsidy-free workforce housing platform
stories.excerpt: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform designed to deliver lower-cost housing without relying on government aid.  The venture targets middle-income renters earning between 80 and 120 percent of the area median income, cutting development costs by handling much of the design, construction and material procurement in-house, according to a news release.  By using a standardized building design and cold-formed steel purchased directly from suppliers, Inception Housing can cut hard construction costs by about 25 percent compared to similar products, PTM Partners CEO Michael Tillman said. The platform’s strategy delivers buildings in six to seven months, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing: subsidy-free workforce housing platform.
Standardized design, cold-formed steel, in-house procurement cut hard costs ~25% vs. traditional; 6–7 month delivery (50% faster).
First project: Magnolia Flats (South Orlando), 74 new units + 14 existing townhomes; targets 80–120% AMI.
stories.summary: PTM Partners (Florida) and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform delivering lower-cost workforce housing (80–120% AMI) without government subsidies. The platform standardizes design and procures cold-formed steel directly from suppliers, cutting hard construction costs ~25% and delivery time to 6–7 months (vs. traditional ~14 months). The first project, Magnolia Flats (South Orlando), combines 14 acquired townhomes with 74 new units. Florida faces a 30.5K-unit housing shortage statewide; live-local and other incentives are complementary.
stories.impact: Inception Housing represents emerging class of cost-engineered, subsidy-free workforce housing using modular/standardized approaches. If replicable and financeable at scale, pressures rents in 80–120% AMI cohort but enables development where public subsidy unavailable. Watch for lending appetite; lower costs and faster delivery may compress multifamily cap rates and increase development pace in Sunbelt secondary markets. South Florida/Orlando supply implications material if model gains capital backing.

stories.title: Dallas apartment pipeline gets a boost with pair of financing deals
stories.excerpt: Two developers of a pair of apartment complexes in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex recently secured financing for their separate projects.  Endeavor Real Estate locked in financing for their luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue named Lucille. Additionally, StreetLights Residential snagged $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano, Texas, which will be built in the redevelopment of The Park at Legacy at 6501 Legacy Drive.  Lucille will feature 265 units across 22 stories in Uptown, according to the Dallas Morning News. The development is helmed by Austin-based Endeavor and equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. Construction on […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate secured financing for Lucille (265 units, 22 stories, Uptown Dallas) at 2700 McKinney Ave.
Construction begins summer 2025; opening late 2028; 320K SF, 10K SF ground-floor retail, rooftop pool, 4-level underground parking.
StreetLights Residential secured $54M equity for 22-story tower in Plano (Legacy redevelopment, 6501 Legacy Drive); 5-yr financing via Northmarq.
stories.summary: Two Dallas-area luxury apartment financing deals: (1) Endeavor Real Estate locked financing for Lucille, a 265-unit, 22-story luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney in Uptown, with partner Canyon Partners; construction begins summer 2025, opening late 2028; property includes 10K SF ground retail, rooftop pool, observation deck, and 4 levels underground parking. (2) StreetLights Residential secured $54 million equity for a 22-story tower in Plano as part of the 107-acre Legacy redevelopment; Northmarq structured the 5-year financing.
stories.impact: Dual Uptown Dallas + Plano financing closures signal strong capital availability and continued confidence in DFW luxury apartment and mixed-use office-to-residential conversion. Uptown Lucille delivery (late 2028) adds 265 units to tight, high-barrier-to-entry submarket; Legacy redevelopment in Plano represents major office-campus-to-mixed-use pivot, unlocking suburban renter demand. Both transactions indicate construction financing still available despite rate environment; development momentum in DFW remains robust. Supply pressure on rents in Uptown and emerging Plano mixed-use rental demand likely.
